title: Caltha palustris lusus subintegerrima
intro: Caltha palustris lusus subintegerrima, also known as Marsh Marigold, is a perennial herbaceous plant belonging to the Ranunculaceae family, characterized by its bright yellow flowers and shiny green leaves. The plant has been traditionally used in herbal medicine for its anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, offering potential health benefits in treating wounds, skin conditions, and respiratory issues. Bioactive constituents of Marsh Marigold include alkaloids, glycosides, and phenolic compounds, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Marsh Marigold is often prepared as a tincture or infused in hot water to make a tea, and is also used in salves and creams for topical applications.
taxonomy: Caltha palustris lusus subintegerrima is a cultivar of marsh marigold, a flowering plant. It belongs to the genus Caltha, which is classified under the family Ranunculaceae. This plant is native to Europe and Asia, but its cultivar classification suggests it has been selectively bred for specific characteristics. As a cultivar, it would not be considered a natural occurrence of the species but rather a human-developed variation. The classification of Caltha palustris lusus subintegerrima would typically be written as Caltha palustris 'Lusus Subintegerrima' in horticultural nomenclature.
uses: Caltha palustris lusus subintegerrima, also known as marsh marigold, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. However, its medicinal uses are not well-documented and require further research. Some studies suggest that the plant may have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, but more studies are needed to confirm its effectiveness. In traditional European folk medicine, the plant was used to treat a range of ailments, including fever and skin conditions.
